findings.message: Destructive action (DELETE) is irreversible and fires immediately on click with no confirmation dialog. A misclick will permanently remove the item with no recovery.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the delete call in a confirmation step (e.g., window.confirm or a modal) before issuing the request, so users can cancel an accidental click.

findings.message: The mutating DELETE action has no feedback while the request is in flight: the button is not disabled, there is no pending/loading indicator, and the user can click repeatedly. On a slow network the UI appears frozen until the page reloads.
findings.suggestion: Track a pending state (e.g., useState<boolean>) and disable the button / show a spinner while the request is in flight to prevent duplicate submissions and give the user feedback.

findings.message: The DELETE fetch has no error handling — failures are swallowed and the page is unconditionally reloaded, so the user is never told the delete failed and may believe a still-present item was deleted.
findings.suggestion: Check response.ok / catch errors and surface a visible error message (and skip the reload on failure) so users get feedback when the action does not succeed.

findings.message: Using window.location.reload() to refresh after delete is a heavy, dead-end UX: it discards client state, causes a visible full-page flash, and provides no in-place success feedback. Users lose scroll position and any context they had.
findings.suggestion: Update local component state (or refetch the items list) on success and show a transient success indication instead of forcing a full page reload.
